headlines.title: Mexico World Cup win draws thousands of fans to the Bay Area
headlines.description: Massive crowds of soccer fans flooded the Bay Area to celebrate Mexico advancing to the Round of 32 in the World Cup following a 1-0 victory over South Korea on Thursday. In San Jose, an estimated 30,000 fans packed San Pedro Square to capacity, heavily outnumbering the previous week’s turnout. The overwhelming attendance prompted a heavy police presence, and security officials restricted access to the main plaza before halftime. The lack of space forced resourceful fans to find alternative viewing angles, with some crowding onto the upper levels of a nearby parking garage. One group even used DoorDash to order a television, a power inverter, and an extension cable to stream the match on the street. “It was crazy, I didn’t think it was going to be this busy,” said Victoria, a San Jose resident. “But it was really fun.” Local businesses felt the surge as well. At The Old Wagon Saloon & Grill, management reported stocking up on nearly two dozen kegs a day to meet the demand. Following the event’s success, San Jose organizers said they are considering closing additional streets to expand the viewing area for the next watch party. Meanwhile, across the bay in San Francisco, thousands of fans filled Thrive City Plaza outside the Chase Center for a free outdoor screening. The event kicked off with a march led by a Mexican banda, six lowrider cars, and San Francisco Mayor Daniel Lurie. The plaza quickly reached capacity, leaving hundreds of fans cheering from outside the gates. While Mexican supporters made up the majority of the crowd, fans of other nations joined the festivities. South Korea fan Andrew Choi said he and his group were embraced by Mexico fans who wanted to take selfies. “It’s so supportive, no hate at all, just love,” Choi said. Esteban Diaz, a visitor from Guadalajara, Mexico, who bought tickets months ago for an upcoming World Cup match in Santa Clara, said he was blown away by the local support. “I am proud for this, I was expecting a much smaller crowd,” Diaz said. Due to the immense fan interest, Chase Center officials announced they will add several other free viewing events at Thrive City for upcoming tournament matches through July 19.

headlines.title: San Jose bars face supply frenzy as thousands of World Cup fans pack San Pedro Square
headlines.description: Thousands of soccer fans are expected to flood downtown San Jose again Thursday evening, bringing an undeniable economic windfall—and a logistical frenzy—to local businesses hosting watch parties for the World Cup match between Mexico and South Korea. With crowds projected to exceed 30,000 people in San Pedro Square, business owners say the intense demand has forced them to completely upend their typical operations. At the Old Wagon Saloon, a popular bar directly facing the watch party plaza, routine inventory schedules have transformed into around-the-clock restocking operations just to keep the taps flowing. “On the first day of Mexico, we learned quickly that we need almost four to five times of what we usually get just for a day,” said Brandon LoDolce, a bartender at the saloon. “After we close, we get kegs in tonight for tomorrow morning. Then when I come in the morning, we’re getting kegs in for the night games, so we are constantly getting shipment.” LoDolce noted that the bar is currently burning through nearly two dozen kegs a day and has doubled its usual orders of tequila cases. “It’s been insane,” he added. Just down the street, neighboring businesses are reporting a similar surge. Owners note the tournament has provided a critical financial lifeline during an otherwise quiet stretch of the year. “It’s very good for business,” said David Mulvehill, a partner at O’Flaherty’s Irish Pub. “June and July can be lean months downtown because no Sharks, college is out, people are on vacations. So this is a huge boost to our businesses and a big economic impact on the community here on San Pedro.” While local merchants reap the financial benefits, they emphasize that the true highlight of the tournament is the atmosphere being generated on the streets, where strangers of differing backgrounds are instantly bonding over a shared love of the game. The celebration is expected to stretch late into the night. While crowds will eventually thin out after the final whistle, workers at many San Pedro Square establishments face long hours ahead, working through the night to prepare for Friday’s slate of games.

headlines.title: DOJ opens civil rights probe into MLB after Giants' Pride Night hat controversy
headlines.description: The Department of Justice is now launching an investigation into MLB following the Giants’ Pride Night controversy. The controversy came after the MLB warned three Giants players, Landen Roupp, JT Brubaker, and Ryan Walker, about writing bible verses on their Pride Night caps. In a letter to MLB commissioner Rob Manfred, Assistant Attorney General Harmeet Dhillon says the DOJ is referring the league to the equal employment opportunity commission. The letter reads: “The Civil Rights Act prohibits MLB and its franchises from unreasonably burdening the rights of players with religious objections to serving as the league’s vehicle for pro-pride messages. Federal law is clear: employers must modify their uniform requirements to reasonably accommodate their employees’ exercise of religion.” “I hate that it’s dividing us, this time of year I hope that we’re unifying people,” said the director of San Francisco Pride Suzanne Ford. Ford says the moment was painful for many LGBTQ Giants fans, especially in a city with deep ties to LGBTQ history. She says the DOJ investigation is only making the situation worse. “it’s so ludicrous that this story is being spun that Christians are being discriminated against,” Ford said. After Pride Night, the Giants released a statement saying “they are proud to support Pride Night and the LGBTQ community” but also respect that individuals may make “personal choices about team activations.” The team also acknowledged the players’ choices caused “pain and anger for many in the LGBTQ community.” NBC Bay Area reached out to the Giants and MLB for comment, but haven’t heard back. State Senator Scott Wiener said the Giants organization did not go far enough in condemning the players’ actions. “It was surprising to me that the Giants put out a pretty bland statement. Put an additional statement saying it’s wrong what these players did,” Wiener said. But Legal Analyst Steven Clark says the MLB could have a fight on its hands when dealing with a potential legal action against the league. “The Trump administration is saying that Major League Baseball violated these players civil rights and in particular their right to religious expression,” he said.

headlines.title: Food and gas prices in the Bay Area could take time to drop after deal with Iran
headlines.description: As the United States and Iran reach an a preliminary deal with to end their war and possibly easy pressure on global energy markerts, experts say prices on items, including groceries, clothing and gas, will take some time to go down. Experts said shoppers are demanding lower prices, and are starting to see them – slowly. “I think at the end of the day when people go to vote in November, it’s not gonna be whether or not there’s a deal, it’s really gonna turn on, did prices actually come down?” Former counsel of the U.S. Senate Finance Committee Rosemary Becchi said. Talk of peace in the Middle East is also bringing lower gas price to the Bay Area, where gas prices are now in the $5 a gallon range instead of $6 or $7. In fact, California saw one of the biggest declines over the last week, but as the country’s average price fell below $4 a gallon for the first time since March, GasBuddy, too, says be patient. “Gas stations will start to pass along the drop in the price of oil today in the next 24 hours, but it may take a couple of days. There may be some catching up to do,” said GasBuddy Head of Petroleum Analaysis Patrick De Haan.

headlines.title: You could already own some SpaceX stocks. Here's how
headlines.description: Elon Musk’s SpaceX has been the talk of Wall Street and Main Street since it went public last Friday. On Thursday, the company experienced its first-ever losing streak. A second consecutive day of losses. It closed the day more than 6% down, but it’s still 30% higher than the original IPO price of $135 per share. Those with a retirement account, like a 401K, might have SpaceX stock, since some funds already include SpaceX or soon will. Scott Budman explains what this means in the video player above.

headlines.title: Man faces hate crime charges after alleged homophobic vandalism in SF's Castro
headlines.description: The San Francisco District Attorney’s Office has filed felony hate crime charges against a man accused of spray-painting homophobic slurs on a Castro neighborhood flower shop and punching a resident who tried to intervene. District Attorney Brooke Jenkins announced several felony charges, including assault, against 39-year-old Hans Herman Haken in connection with the May 16 incident. Prosecutors are also alleging that the felonies constitute hate crimes. Haken was arraigned on the charges Thursday and ordered to remain in custody until a preliminary hearing next month. According to authorities and witnesses, the incident began outside Chartreuse by Rojé, a flower shop in the city’s prominent LGBTQ+ neighborhood. Jeffrey Dumlao, the shop’s owner, said a white Cadillac Escalade belonging to Haken had been parked outside the storefront for hours. At some point, an unknown individual allegedly threw eggs at the vehicle’s windshield. The vandalism apparently angered Haken, who allegedly began spray-painting a homophobic message on the shop’s exterior wall. Justin Donnelly, who lives above the flower shop, heard the commotion from his apartment. “I heard the sound of a spray paint can shaking and saw him spray painting on the wall,” Donnelly said. “And I decided for whatever reason that I wasn’t going to put up with that.” When Donnelly asked him to stop, Haken allegedly confronted him and accused him of throwing the eggs. Dumlao noted that bystanders defended Donnelly at the scene, telling Haken that Donnelly was not involved in the egging. Donnelly said he took a photograph of the SUV to report it to the police, which prompted Haken to punch him. Haken then sped away, striking a parked car as he fled. Both Dumlao and Donnelly expressed relief over the criminal charges and gratitude for the community’s swift response in helping identify the suspect. “I’m just really grateful that the District Attorney’s office and the police are taking this really seriously,” Donnelly said. “The fact is that something like this can happen anywhere. But, in San Francisco we don’t stand for it. And we deal with it, so that makes me feel good.” Jenkins reiterated the city’s stance on hate-motivated violence following the arraignment. “This is a city where we protect our residents,” Jenkins said. “We protect our visitors. And we will do everything that is necessary to do so. And I think this investigation proves that, I think our charges prove that.”

headlines.title: Crumbling roads, traffic cost Bay Area drivers thousands annually, report finds
headlines.description: Crumbling roads and heavy traffic congestion are costing Bay Area motorists thousands of dollars annually in vehicle repairs, wasted fuel and lost time, according to a report released Thursday by a national transportation research nonprofit. The study, conducted by Washington, D.C.-based TRIP, found that deficient roads and traffic delays cost the average Bay Area driver more than $4,600 each year. In the San Jose metropolitan area, those annual costs average nearly $3,300 per motorist. The report highlights growing concerns over how California will maintain its infrastructure as traditional funding streams begin to dry up. “The Bay Area is facing some of the most complex transportation challenges in the state — from congestion, to climate resilience, to major regional mega-projects,” said Assemblymember Lori Wilson, D-Suisun City. “Meeting those challenges requires stable, long-term funding.” However, securing that funding is becoming increasingly difficult for state and local officials. The federal Bipartisan Infrastructure Law, which currently provides billions of dollars for highways, public transit, and rail systems, is set to expire at the end of September. At the state level, California’s gas tax revenue is steadily declining as more drivers transition to electric and hybrid vehicles. Compounding the revenue shortfall, officials noted that overall construction costs continue to rise, leaving lawmakers looking for alternative ways to fund critical highway and road repairs.

headlines.title: Mother and child left in critical condition after being swept by ‘sneaker wave' in SF
headlines.description: A mother and her child was taken to a hospital in critical condition Thursday afternoon after they were hit by a sneaker wave and pulled out into the surf at San Francisco’s Baker Beach, officials said. The San Francisco Fire Department said bystanders pulled the mother and child out of the water before emergency crews arrived. SFFD released the following statement: “This incident is a stark reminder of the dangers of sneaker waves along San Francisco’s coastline and throughout California. Sneaker waves can surge much farther up the beach than expected, without warning. Never turn your back to the ocean. Stay alert, keep children and pets close, and avoid wet sand and driftwood near the waterline. If you see someone in trouble, call 911 immediately and do not enter the water yourself.”

headlines.title: Man charged in Berkeley animal shelter arson appears in court
headlines.description: A 48-year-old man accused of breaking into and setting fire to the Berkeley Animal Services Shelter appeared in court Thursday. Peter Taraba faces multiple charges, including felony arson, from the June 6 incident. Police said video surveillance footage showed Taraba coming to the animal shelter, breaking a window and using an “improvised incendiary device” to start the fire. According to shelter employees, there were more than 40 animals located in the shelter, including Taraba’s own dog. Long-term pet owners and residents of Berkeley said they are glad no one was hurt and that the shelter is undergoing repairs. Crews continue to repair fire, smoke and water damages at the shelter following the break-in. The shelter remains closed and is expected to reopen on July 6. Taraba is currently jailed in Alameda County, with his bail set at $25,000. Court records also show Taraba faces charges for allegedly assaulting a homeless shelter worker in March, and with vandalism on June 5.

headlines.title: ‘7X7': New San Francisco art installation to feature 49 beams of light
headlines.description: San Francisco Mayor Daniel Lurie on Thursday unveiled a new art installation that will light up the sky in the coming weeks. Produced by Illuminate, the “7X7” installation featuring 49 beams of light will operate from June 21 through July 4 as part of Lurie’s mission to drive more people to the city. “As our city welcomes people from around the world this summer for Pride, July Fourth, and the World Cup, we are showing the creativity, energy, and spirit that make San Francisco the greatest city in the world,” Lurie said in a statement. The installation, located at Civic Center Plaza, is free to the public. Residents and visitors are encouraged to stop by and interact with the piece. “7X7” is set to display rainbow colors for Pride weekend and American flag colors for the United States’ 250th birthday on July 4. “Projects like ‘7X7’ bring people together, activate our civic spaces, and create experiences that are free and accessible to everyone,” San Francisco Recreation and Park Department General Manager Sarah Madland said in a statement. The exhibition is open for all audiences, but only for a limited time.

headlines.title: Brush fire burns 5 acres in hills near Fremont
headlines.description: A brush fire scorched five acres in the hills east of Fremont Thursday afternoon, officials said. The blaze broke out east of the 42000 block of Vargas Road, Cal Fire said. Firefighters were able to stop the fire’s forward progress. Additional information wasn’t immediately available. This story uses functionality that may not work in our app. Click here to open the story in your web browser.

headlines.title: Former UC Davis soccer player Max Arfsten makes it to USMNT
headlines.description: A former UC Davis soccer player is representing the United States on the world stage. Max Arfsten is one of 26 players on Team USA, and his journey is unlike most of his teammates. The 25-year-old Fresno native plays in major league soccer for the Columbus Crew as a mid-fielder. He attended UC Davis as a walk-on and ended up being named to the Big West All-Freshman team in 2019. That same year, he helped the Aggies win the conference title. Arfsten describes his time at UC Davis as amazing. “I’m still good friends with so many people I went to school there with. That have reached out to me and told me they’re so happy for me. They think it’s crazy that I was at UC Davis and now I’m on this stage. So, those types of interactions and conversations are really what it’s all about. People who knew me then, following my journey now it’s very rewarding for me,” he said. He also credits his coaches for his success and work ethic. His former coach, Dwayne Shaffer, joined Raj Mathai to talk about his time with the player. Watch the full interview in the video above. USMNT Jun 17 2026 World Cup Jun 17 VTA Jun 18 World Cup brings record ridership and long lines for VTA in Santa Clara
